title: (SACRAMENTO, CA) –Assemblymember Tina McKinnor (D - Inglewood) today introduced Assembly Bill 1628, which will require a microfiber filter on all new washing machines sold in Calif
prompt_preview:

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "When clothes containing synthetic fibers are washed, they shed plastic microfibers that end up in freshwater systems and the ocean.",
    "Microfibers can be found in tap water, bottled water, and food products including fish, table salt, and beer.",
    "Microfibers captured by wastewater treatment plants can be reintroduced to the environment through sewage sludge as fertilizer.",
    "Microfibers can persist in California’s agricultural fields for decades, potentially compromising food production and posing health risks to consumers.",
    "The rise of fast fashion has increased the production of clothes made from synthetic materials, resulting in more microfibers being released into oceans and freshwater.",
    "Research has demonstrated that microfiber pollution is a significant threat to Californians, with at least 4.85 million lbs. of fibers entering lands and waters each year.",
    "Mandating the use of filters in washing machines can be an effective solution to limit microfiber emissions.",
    "Microfibers are the most common form of microplastic pollution in the environment.",
    "A single load of synthetic laundry can release up to 18 million microfibers into the environment.",
    "Adding filters to washing machines can drastically reduce the number of microfibers released during laundry.",
    "Filters in washing machines are effective at capturing up to 90% of microfibers."
  ]
}
```
